title: DagNet Shared Settings
description:
Shared (repo-committed) settings that affect analytics and forecasting behaviour across clients. These settings are intended to be version-controlled and pulled from git so results are consistent across machines.

Important: changing these values changes computed results. Treat changes like code changes: coordinate, commit, and communicate.

version.title: Schema Version
version.description: Settings file schema version. Normally you should not change this.

forecasting.title: Forecasting Settings
forecasting.description:
Knobs controlling DagNet's forecasting and latency-aware blending.

These affect all fetches and analyses, so treat changes like code changes: commit them and communicate them.

For full explanations and worked examples, see Help → Forecasting settings.

RECENCY_HALF_LIFE_DAYS.title: Recency Half-Life (days)
RECENCY_HALF_LIFE_DAYS.description:
Controls how strongly *recent* (mature) cohorts are weighted when estimating the baseline forecast probability.

Definition: a mature cohort that is H days older has half the weight of a brand-new mature cohort (exponential half-life).

Effects:
- Higher values = more stable / slower-moving baseline (older mature data still matters).
- Lower values = more responsive baseline (recent mature data dominates).

Indicative values:
- 7–14: very responsive (use when conversion genuinely shifts week-to-week)
- 30: balanced (default)
- 60–90: conservative (use when rates are stable and you want less noise)
- Very large (e.g. 10^7): effectively 'no recency' (mature-day k/n dominates without age weighting).

LATENCY_MIN_EFFECTIVE_SAMPLE_SIZE.title: Minimum Effective Sample Size
LATENCY_MIN_EFFECTIVE_SAMPLE_SIZE.description:
A stability guardrail for recency-weighted baselines.

When recency weighting is applied, the *effective* sample size (N_eff) can be much smaller than the raw count because older cohorts contribute less weight. If N_eff drops below this threshold, the system uses a more conservative strategy to avoid unstable baselines.

Indicative values:
- 50–150: allows more responsiveness, but can be noisy on thin data
- 150–500: typical range (default 150)
- 500+: very conservative; expect slower reaction to real change.

DEFAULT_T95_DAYS.title: Default t95 (days)
DEFAULT_T95_DAYS.description:
Fallback maturity horizon (in days) when an edge has no reliable latency estimate.

This determines how much of the most recent data is treated as potentially incomplete ('immature') for lagged conversions.

Indicative values:
- 0–2: only appropriate if latency is genuinely near-instant and you have high confidence
- 7–21: typical for many behavioural funnels
- 30: conservative default
- 60–90: very conservative; reduces risk of counting immature conversions as missing, but increases lag in responsiveness.

FORECAST_BLEND_LAMBDA.title: Forecast Blend λ (lambda)
FORECAST_BLEND_LAMBDA.description:
Controls how strongly the forecast baseline acts as a prior when blending forecast and evidence in immature data.

Higher values keep p.mean closer to the forecast for longer (more 'prior strength'). Lower values let evidence dominate sooner.

Indicative values:
- 0.05–0.20: typical range (default 0.15)
- 0.20–0.50: more conservative (forecast dominates longer)
- 0: disables the prior entirely (not recommended unless you really want raw evidence).

LATENCY_BLEND_COMPLETENESS_POWER.title: Completeness Power η (eta)
LATENCY_BLEND_COMPLETENESS_POWER.description:
Controls how aggressively completeness is down-weighted when computing the blend weight.

Values > 1 make the system more conservative (less willing to trust evidence when completeness is below 1). Values = 1 preserve the canonical formula.

Indicative values:
- 1.0: canonical behaviour (no extra conservatism)
- 1.5–3.0: typical 'safety' range (default 2.25)
- 3.0–5.0: very conservative; expect p.mean to cling to forecast longer for immature cohorts.

ANCHOR_DELAY_BLEND_K_CONVERSIONS.title: Anchor Delay Blend K (conversions)
ANCHOR_DELAY_BLEND_K_CONVERSIONS.description:
Controls how quickly *observed* anchor-delay evidence overrides the *prior* anchor delay when computing cohort-mode completeness on downstream edges.

This is a credibility / sample-size knob: the blend weight grows roughly like (1 - exp(-x/K)), where x is the effective number of forecast conversions supporting the observed anchor delay.

Lower values make the system trust observed anchor lag sooner; higher values keep the upstream-derived prior anchor delay longer.

Indicative values:
- 20–50: more responsive (trust observed anchor lag quickly)
- 50–150: typical range (default 50)
- 150–300: more conservative (prior dominates longer; reduces instability when anchor lag is sparse).

ONSET_MASS_FRACTION_ALPHA.title: Onset Mass Fraction α
ONSET_MASS_FRACTION_ALPHA.description:
The cumulative mass fraction used to derive onset from a window() lag histogram.

Onset is defined as the earliest day by which this fraction of total conversion mass has occurred. The result is stored as onset_delta_days (rounded to 1 d.p.) and used to left-shift the lognormal fit so it starts from the onset day rather than day 0.

Lower values produce an earlier onset (more aggressive shifting), which can improve fit quality for edges with a genuine minimum delay. Higher values are more conservative.

Indicative values:
- 0.005: very aggressive (use with caution — sensitive to noise)
- 0.01: balanced default
- 0.02–0.05: conservative (onset shifts less).

ONSET_AGGREGATION_BETA.title: Onset Aggregation β
ONSET_AGGREGATION_BETA.description:
Quantile used when aggregating onset values across multiple window() slice families in the topological LAG pass. Each slice family contributes one onset value, weighted by its series length (dates.length).

β=0.5 is a weighted median, which provides robust aggregation that isn't distorted by a single outlier slice.

Indicative values:
- 0.25: biased toward earlier onset (more aggressive shifting)
- 0.5: robust median (default)
- 0.75: biased toward later onset (more conservative).

LATENCY_MAX_MEAN_MEDIAN_RATIO.title: Max Mean/Median Ratio
LATENCY_MAX_MEAN_MEDIAN_RATIO.description:
Guardrail for lognormal moment-based fitting. When fitting σ from the mean/median ratio, extremely large ratios can produce unrealistically wide distributions.

With onset shifting enabled, medianX can become very small while the histogram still has a real tail, making the ratio legitimately large. Setting this very large (default 999999) effectively disables the guardrail, letting σ be inferred from moments even for heavy-tailed cases.

Lower values (e.g. 3–10) enforce a skew limit, which can suppress genuine tails.

Indicative values:
- 3–10: moderate skew limit (use if you see pathological σ values)
- 999999: guardrail disabled (default — recommended with onset shifting).

BAYES_LOG_KAPPA_MU.title: log(κ) Prior Centre
BAYES_LOG_KAPPA_MU.description:
Centre of the LogNormal prior on κ (the overdispersion / concentration parameter).

κ controls how much day-to-day conversion rates are allowed to vary around the edge mean. Higher κ = less variation (more concentrated around the mean); lower κ = more variation.

The prior is placed on log(κ), so this value is in log-space. log(30) ≈ 3.4 means the prior expects κ ≈ 30, which allows moderate day-to-day variation.

Indicative values:
- 2.3 (κ ≈ 10): expects substantial overdispersion
- 3.4 (κ ≈ 30): moderate expectation (default)
- 4.6 (κ ≈ 100): expects low overdispersion.

BAYES_LOG_KAPPA_SIGMA.title: log(κ) Prior Width
BAYES_LOG_KAPPA_SIGMA.description:
Width (standard deviation) of the LogNormal prior on κ.

Controls how uncertain the model is about the overdispersion level before seeing data. Wider priors allow the data to dominate; narrower priors constrain κ more tightly.

At 1.5, the 95% credible interval for κ is roughly [2, 500], which is broad enough for most conversion funnels.

Indicative values:
- 0.5: tight prior (strong belief about κ level)
- 1.5: broad prior (default — lets data determine κ)
- 3.0: very broad (essentially non-informative).

BAYES_FALLBACK_PRIOR_ESS.title: Fallback Prior ESS
BAYES_FALLBACK_PRIOR_ESS.description:
Effective sample size for the fallback Beta prior, used when no Phase 1 posterior is available for an edge.

When the Bayesian compiler has no warm-start posterior (e.g. first fit, or posterior was reset), it constructs a Beta(α, β) prior from the frequentist p.mean with this ESS. Higher ESS = stronger prior (more data needed to shift it); lower ESS = weaker prior.

Indicative values:
- 5–10: weak prior (data dominates quickly)
- 20: moderate (default)
- 50–100: strong prior (useful for very stable edges).

BAYES_DIRICHLET_CONC_FLOOR.title: Dirichlet Concentration Floor
BAYES_DIRICHLET_CONC_FLOOR.description:
Minimum concentration parameter for the Beta/Dirichlet prior.

Prevents degenerate priors where α or β would be less than this floor. The value 0.5 corresponds to the Jeffreys prior (non-informative), which is appropriate when you have no strong prior belief.

Values below 0.5 produce U-shaped priors favouring extreme probabilities; values above 0.5 produce priors favouring moderate probabilities.

Indicative values:
- 0.5: Jeffreys prior (default)
- 1.0: uniform prior
- 2.0: mildly informative toward moderate values.

BAYES_SIGMA_FLOOR.title: Latency σ Floor
BAYES_SIGMA_FLOOR.description:
Minimum lognormal σ for latency modelling.

If the fitted σ falls below this floor, the edge is treated as having effectively instant conversion (no meaningful latency spread). This prevents the model from trying to fit a near-delta-function latency distribution, which can cause numerical instability.

Indicative values:
- 0.01: tight floor (default — only truly instant edges are floored)
- 0.1: looser floor (more edges treated as instant)
- 0.5: very loose (would floor edges with real latency spread).

BAYES_MU_PRIOR_SIGMA_FLOOR.title: μ Prior Sigma Floor
BAYES_MU_PRIOR_SIGMA_FLOOR.description:
Minimum uncertainty (standard deviation) on the latency μ prior.

Provides exploration room for the MCMC sampler when the frequentist μ estimate is very precise. Without this floor, the sampler could get stuck near a tight prior and fail to explore the posterior properly.

Indicative values:
- 0.1: allows very tight priors (use if you trust the frequentist estimate)
- 0.5: moderate exploration room (default)
- 1.0: generous exploration (use if fits are not converging).

BAYES_MATURITY_FLOOR.title: Maturity Floor
BAYES_MATURITY_FLOOR.description:
Minimum CDF completeness required for an observation to contribute to overdispersion (κ) estimation.

Immature observations (low completeness) have systematically biased conversion rates, which would distort κ estimation if included. This floor excludes observations below the threshold.

Indicative values:
- 0.8: slightly more inclusive (may add noise from semi-mature data)
- 0.9: balanced (default)
- 0.95: strict (only near-fully-mature data contributes).

BAYES_SOFTPLUS_SHARPNESS.title: Softplus Onset Sharpness
BAYES_SOFTPLUS_SHARPNESS.description:
Sharpness of the softplus function used to model the onset boundary in the Bayesian latency likelihood.

Higher values produce a sharper cutoff below the onset day (closer to a step function); lower values produce a smoother transition. The softplus avoids the discontinuity of a hard cutoff, which improves MCMC sampling stability.

Indicative values:
- 1–2: very smooth transition
- 5: moderate sharpness
- 8: default (raised from 5 on 2-Apr-26 for better NUTS warmup stability)
- 10–20: very sharp (close to a hard cutoff).

BAYES_RHAT_THRESHOLD.title: R-hat Threshold
BAYES_RHAT_THRESHOLD.description:
Maximum Gelman-Rubin R-hat statistic for declaring MCMC convergence.

R-hat compares between-chain and within-chain variance. Values near 1.0 indicate that all chains have converged to the same distribution. Fits exceeding this threshold are flagged as potentially unreliable.

Indicative values:
- 1.01: strict (requires very good convergence)
- 1.05: standard threshold (default)
- 1.10: lenient (accepts fits with some between-chain disagreement).

BAYES_ESS_THRESHOLD.title: Min ESS
BAYES_ESS_THRESHOLD.description:
Minimum effective sample size (ESS) for declaring MCMC convergence.

ESS measures how many independent samples the chains are equivalent to after accounting for autocorrelation. Higher ESS = more precise posterior estimates.

Indicative values:
- 100–200: acceptable for quick fits
- 400: standard (default)
- 1000+: high precision (slower fitting).

BAYES_WARM_START_RHAT_MAX.title: Warm-Start R-hat Max
BAYES_WARM_START_RHAT_MAX.description:
Quality gate for accepting a previous posterior as a warm-start for the next fit.

If the previous fit's R-hat exceeds this threshold, warm-starting is rejected and the fit starts from the prior instead. This prevents a bad previous fit from contaminating subsequent fits.

Typically set looser than BAYES_RHAT_THRESHOLD since warm-starts are still useful even from slightly imperfect fits.

Indicative values:
- 1.05: strict (only very well-converged posteriors used as warm-start)
- 1.10: standard (default)
- 1.20: lenient (accepts moderately converged posteriors).

BAYES_WARM_START_ESS_MIN.title: Warm-Start Min ESS
BAYES_WARM_START_ESS_MIN.description:
ESS quality gate for warm-starting. If the previous fit's ESS is below this, the warm-start is rejected.

Indicative values:
- 50: lenient (most fits accepted)
- 100: standard (default)
- 200: stricter.

BAYES_HDI_PROB.title: HDI Probability
BAYES_HDI_PROB.description:
Probability mass for the Highest Density Interval (HDI) reported in fit results.

The HDI is the narrowest interval containing this fraction of the posterior probability. It appears in the Bayes fit diagnostics and is used for credible interval display.

Indicative values:
- 0.80: 80% HDI (narrower, less conservative)
- 0.90: 90% HDI (default)
- 0.95: 95% HDI (wider, more conservative).

BAYES_DRAWS.title: Draws per Chain
BAYES_DRAWS.description:
Number of MCMC draws retained per chain after warmup.

Total posterior samples = DRAWS × CHAINS. More draws improve the accuracy of posterior summaries (means, HDIs) but increase compute time linearly.

Indicative values:
- 500: fast exploratory fits
- 2000: standard (default)
- 5000–10000: high-precision fits (e.g. for publication-quality diagnostics).

BAYES_TUNE.title: Warmup per Chain
BAYES_TUNE.description:
Number of warmup (tuning) iterations per chain. These samples are discarded — their purpose is to adapt the sampler's step size and mass matrix.

Insufficient warmup can cause poor mixing and low ESS. More warmup rarely hurts but adds compute time.

Indicative values:
- 500: fast (adequate for simple models)
- 1000: standard (default)
- 2000–5000: generous (use if fits show low ESS or high R-hat).

BAYES_CHAINS.title: Number of Chains
BAYES_CHAINS.description:
Number of independent MCMC chains run in parallel. Multiple chains are essential for R-hat convergence diagnostics — with only 1 chain, R-hat cannot be computed.

More chains also improve ESS (effective sample size scales with chain count) but multiply compute time.

Indicative values:
- 2: minimum for R-hat (fast, lower ESS)
- 4: standard (default — good balance of diagnostics and speed)
- 8: high reliability (use for critical fits where convergence assurance matters).

BAYES_TARGET_ACCEPT.title: NUTS Target Accept
BAYES_TARGET_ACCEPT.description:
Target acceptance rate for the NUTS (No-U-Turn Sampler) algorithm.

Higher values make the sampler take smaller, more conservative steps, reducing divergences but slowing exploration. Lower values allow larger steps, which can improve mixing but may cause divergences in complex posteriors.

Indicative values:
- 0.80: aggressive (faster exploration, more divergence risk)
- 0.90: standard (default)
- 0.95–0.99: very conservative (use if seeing many divergences).

development.title: Development Settings (Local)
development.description: Local-only development toggles. These should generally not be committed into a shared settings file.

debugGitOperations.title: Debug Git Operations
debugGitOperations.description: Enable detailed logging for Git operations.
